flashcards.front: What are the three primary file access methods?
flashcards.back: 1. Sequential access 2. Direct (Random) access 3. Indexed access

flashcards.front: Define sequential access method
flashcards.back: Data is accessed in linear sequence from beginning to end. Common in tape drives and media streaming.

flashcards.front: How does direct access differ from sequential?
flashcards.back: Direct access allows reading/writing records in any order using addresses, while sequential requires linear access.

flashcards.front: What is the main advantage of indexed access?
flashcards.back: Enables fast record retrieval using key values through a separate index structure, ideal for database systems.

flashcards.front: Formula for direct access block location
flashcards.back: Block_address = (Block_number × Block_size) + Offset

flashcards.front: Real-world example of sequential access
flashcards.back: Magnetic tape drives, log files processing, video streaming

flashcards.front: Purpose of file pointer in sequential access
flashcards.back: Tracks current read/write position and automatically advances after each operation.

flashcards.front: Two implementations of direct access
flashcards.back: 1. Relative record numbers 2. Hashed addressing using key values

flashcards.front: What problem does indexed sequential access solve?
flashcards.back: Combines sequential ordering with direct access through indexes (e.g., ISAM - Indexed Sequential Access Method)

flashcards.front: Key components of indexed access
flashcards.back: 1. Primary data file 2. Index file (key + pointer pairs) 3. Overflow area for new records

flashcards.front: OS system call for direct access
flashcards.back: lseek() - Repositions file offset for next read/write operation

flashcards.front: Disadvantage of sequential access
flashcards.back: Inefficient for random access needs - O(n) time complexity for arbitrary data retrieval

flashcards.front: How indexed access handles record deletion
flashcards.back: Mark records as deleted in index, periodically reorganize files to reclaim space

flashcards.front: Example of hybrid access method
flashcards.back: Indexed sequential (ISAM) - Combines sequential storage with index-based direct access

flashcards.front: Impact of block size on direct access
flashcards.back: Larger blocks reduce storage overhead but increase internal fragmentation

flashcards.front: Role of file allocation table in access methods
flashcards.back: Maps logical block numbers to physical disk locations for both sequential and direct access

flashcards.front: Why are direct access files typically fixed-length?
flashcards.back: Enables O(1) access time using arithmetic offset calculation (Block_number × Block_size)

flashcards.front: Three types of indexes in indexed access
flashcards.back: 1. Primary index 2. Secondary index 3. Clustered index

flashcards.front: Access method used in database B+ trees
flashcards.back: Indexed access using tree structures for efficient range queries and key-based retrieval

flashcards.front: How fragmentation affects direct access
flashcards.back: External fragmentation increases seek time, requiring periodic file compaction

flashcards.front: Security consideration in access methods
flashcards.back: Access methods work with OS permissions - e.g., direct access requires read/write privilege checks

flashcards.front: Comparison: RAM vs File access methods
flashcards.back: RAM uses direct access only, files support multiple methods depending on storage medium

flashcards.front: When to use sequential access?
flashcards.back: When processing entire files linearly (e.g., backups, batch processing, multimedia streaming)

flashcards.front: Advantage of indexed over direct access
flashcards.back: Efficient handling of variable-length records and secondary key searches

flashcards.front: How modern OS combine access methods
flashcards.back: Provide low-level block access (direct) while supporting higher-level indexed/sequential APIs
